title: Paneer tikka masala
subtitle: Grilled paneer cubes in tomato and cream sauce
excerpt: Paneer cubes marinated in spiced yoghurt and char-grilled until the surface chars, then simmered in the same tomato, cream, and butter sauce as chicken tikka masala. The paneer holds its texture in the sauce where chicken would soften — the contrast between the slightly firm, charred exterior and the smooth, rich sauce is the dish's defining quality. The vegetarian answer to chicken tikka masala.
sourceNotes: Paneer tikka masala is the vegetarian parallel to chicken tikka masala, applying the same tikka-and-masala-sauce structure to grilled paneer. It has been on British Indian menus since the 1980s and is consistently the most popular vegetarian main for non-vegetarian diners.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the sauce ahead. Grill the paneer fresh and add to the reheated sauce.

content.text: Paneer behaves differently from chicken in the tikka stage. It doesn't absorb marinade in the same way — the marinade coats the surface rather than penetrating the interior. This means the char on the outside is more important for paneer than for chicken: the charred, spiced surface is the main flavour contribution the tikka stage makes, and it must be properly dark and caramelised to have impact in the finished sauce.

The sauce is the chicken tikka masala sauce with no modifications. Use the same formula: caramelised onion base, tomatoes reduced to a paste, butter, cream, and fenugreek leaves. The paneer goes in at the end, just long enough to warm through — it should not braise in the sauce for more than 5 minutes, or it will become rubbery.

Combine the {{plain-yoghurt}}, {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{garam-masala}}, {{chilli-powder}}, {{turmeric}}, {{vegetable-oil}}, and a pinch of salt. Add the {{paneer}} cubes and turn to coat. Leave for at least 1 hour. Preheat a cast-iron griddle pan until smoking. Grill the paneer for 2 to 3 minutes per side until well charred in spots. Remove and set aside.

Heat the {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 10 minutes until golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{coriander-ground}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 10 minutes until reduced to a thick paste. Add 100 ml water and simmer for 5 minutes. Stir in the {{double-cream}} and {{fenugreek-leaves}}. Season with salt. Add the grilled paneer and warm through on low heat for 3 to 4 minutes. Serve immediately.

items.cause: Kept too long in the hot sauce
items.fix: Add paneer to the finished sauce off the heat, or on the very lowest setting. It needs only to warm through — not to cook further

items.symptom: Paneer doesn't char on the griddle
items.cause: Pan not hot enough or too much marinade left on
items.fix: Wipe excess marinade from the paneer before grilling — a thick layer prevents direct contact with the pan. The griddle must be smoking

items.symptom: Sauce tastes identical to chicken tikka masala
items.cause: It is the same sauce — the difference is in the paneer
items.fix: The sauces are the same. The textural contrast (firm paneer vs. chicken) and the charred paneer flavour are what differentiate the dish

items.symptom: Paneer falls apart on the griddle
items.cause: Cut too small or fresh paneer not firm enough
items.fix: Cut paneer into 3 cm cubes minimum. If using very fresh soft paneer, press between two plates for 30 minutes before cutting to firm it

content.text: Paneer tikka masala is the vegetarian main for curry-house diners who want the tikka masala experience without chicken. It occupies the same position on the menu as saag paneer (the vegetarian centrepiece) but appeals to a different preference: those who want a rich, sauced main rather than the spinach and cream alternative. The charred paneer surface gives it more textural interest than a plain paneer curry, and the char flavour integrates with the cream sauce in a way that plain fried paneer does not.
